standard.notes: 'Allez tout droit' = Go straight ahead. One of the most essential direction phrases in French. 'Tout droit' is invariable — it never changes form. Often paired with 'puis' (then) to string directions together: 'Allez tout droit, puis tournez à gauche.'
examples: Continuez tout droit jusqu'au feu rouge.
Continue straight ahead to the traffic lights.
La gare est tout droit, à cinq minutes.
The station is straight ahead, five minutes away.

standard.meaning: to the left / on the left
standard.notes: 'Tournez à gauche' = Turn left. 'À gauche du cinéma' = to the left of the cinema. In France, 'la gauche' also refers to the political left. On the road, remember that France drives on the right, so 'à gauche' at a junction means crossing oncoming traffic.
examples: Tournez à gauche au carrefour.
Turn left at the crossroads.
La pharmacie est à gauche.
The pharmacy is on the left.

standard.meaning: to the right / on the right
standard.notes: 'Tournez à droite' = Turn right. 'À droite de la boulangerie' = to the right of the bakery. Politically, 'la droite' = the political right. Always used with 'à' — never say just 'droite' alone when giving directions.
examples: Prenez la première rue à droite.
Take the first street on the right.
Le musée est à droite après le pont.
The museum is on the right after the bridge.

standard.notes: 'Un plan de la ville' = a city map. Distinct from 'la carte' (a larger geographical map or a menu). 'Vous avez un plan ?' = Do you have a map? In French cities, tourist offices ('offices de tourisme') give out free 'plans' of the city centre.
examples: Vous avez un plan de la ville ?
Do you have a city map?
Regardons le plan — la gare est ici.
Let's look at the map — the station is here.

standard.notes: 'Dans quelle direction ?' = In which direction? 'La direction de Paris' = the direction of Paris. Also used on road signs: 'Direction centre-ville' = towards the town centre. In a company context, 'la direction' means management/leadership.
examples: Dans quelle direction est la gare ?
In which direction is the station?
Suivez la direction centre-ville.
Follow the direction to the town centre.

advanced.notes: 'Allez tout droit' = Go straight ahead. One of the most essential direction phrases in French. 'Tout droit' is invariable — it never changes form. Often paired with 'puis' (then) to string directions together: 'Allez tout droit, puis tournez à gauche.'
examples: Continuez tout droit jusqu'au feu rouge.
Continue straight ahead to the traffic lights.
La gare est tout droit, à cinq minutes.
The station is straight ahead, five minutes away.

advanced.meaning: to the left / on the left
advanced.notes: 'Tournez à gauche' = Turn left. 'À gauche du cinéma' = to the left of the cinema. In France, 'la gauche' also refers to the political left. On the road, remember that France drives on the right, so 'à gauche' at a junction means crossing oncoming traffic.
examples: Tournez à gauche au carrefour.
Turn left at the crossroads.
La pharmacie est à gauche.
The pharmacy is on the left.

advanced.meaning: to the right / on the right
advanced.notes: 'Tournez à droite' = Turn right. 'À droite de la boulangerie' = to the right of the bakery. Politically, 'la droite' = the political right. Always used with 'à' — never say just 'droite' alone when giving directions.
examples: Prenez la première rue à droite.
Take the first street on the right.
Le musée est à droite après le pont.
The museum is on the right after the bridge.

advanced.notes: 'Un plan de la ville' = a city map. Distinct from 'la carte' (a larger geographical map or a menu). 'Vous avez un plan ?' = Do you have a map? In French cities, tourist offices ('offices de tourisme') give out free 'plans' of the city centre.
examples: Vous avez un plan de la ville ?
Do you have a city map?
Regardons le plan — la gare est ici.
Let's look at the map — the station is here.

advanced.notes: 'Dans quelle direction ?' = In which direction? 'La direction de Paris' = the direction of Paris. Also used on road signs: 'Direction centre-ville' = towards the town centre. In a company context, 'la direction' means management/leadership.
examples: Dans quelle direction est la gare ?
In which direction is the station?
Suivez la direction centre-ville.
Follow the direction to the town centre.

advanced.notes: Regular -er verb. 'Traverser la rue' = to cross the street. 'Traverser le pont' = to cross the bridge. Essential for giving directions: 'Traversez la place, puis tournez à gauche.' Also used figuratively: 'traverser une période difficile' = to go through a difficult period.
examples: Traversez le pont et tournez à droite.
Cross the bridge and turn right.
Il traverse la rue au feu piéton.
He crosses the street at the pedestrian light.

advanced.notes: Regular -er verb. 'Continuez tout droit' = Continue straight ahead. 'Continuer sur cette route' = to continue on this road. Very common in directions. 'Continuez jusqu'à…' = Continue as far as / until… ('jusqu'à' means 'as far as / until').
examples: Continuez tout droit pendant deux cents mètres.
Continue straight ahead for two hundred metres.
Vous continuez jusqu'au rond-point.
You continue as far as the roundabout.

dialogue: Excusez-moi, je viens d'arriver en ville. Où est la gare, s'il vous plaît ?
La gare ? Vous allez tout droit jusqu'au carrefour.
Ensuite, vous tournez à gauche. La gare est à droite, après deux cents mètres.
À gauche, puis à droite. Je comprends. Vous avez un plan ?
Désolé, je n'ai pas de plan. Mais c'est simple — dix minutes à pied.
Dans quelle direction exactement ? Nord, sud ?
Vers le nord. Suivez la direction 'Gare centrale'.

questions.question: What does the tourist say about their arrival?
options: They have just arrived in town
questions.correctAnswer: They have just arrived in town

questions.question: Which direction does the tourist turn at the crossroads?
How long does the local say the walk takes?

standard.summary: A tourist who has just arrived in town asks a local how to reach the train station. The local instructs them to go straight ahead to the crossroads, then turn left, and the station will be on the right after 200 metres. The local doesn't have a map but assures the tourist it is a ten-minute walk heading north.

dialogue: Pardon — je viens d'arriver et je cherche la pharmacie la plus proche.
La pharmacie ? Vous venez d'où — de la gare ?
Oui, je viens de la gare. J'ai marché cinq minutes.
D'accord. Continuez tout droit jusqu'au feu rouge, puis traversez la place.
Je traverse la place — et ensuite ?
Ensuite, prenez la direction du marché. Tournez à droite au plan de la ville.
À droite au plan — c'est loin ?
Non, la pharmacie est à gauche, à deux minutes. Vous ne pouvez pas la manquer.

questions.question: Where has the tourist just come from?
What does the tourist need to cross?
Which direction does the tourist turn at the town map?
How far away is the pharmacy at the end of the directions?

advanced.summary: A tourist who has just arrived (from the train station) asks a local for the nearest pharmacy. The local uses 'venir de' to clarify the starting point, then gives step-by-step directions: go straight ahead to the traffic lights, cross the square, take the direction of the market, turn right at the town map, and the pharmacy is on the left — about two minutes away.

standard.title: Venir (Present Tense — Conjugation & Common Uses)
standard.explanation:
**Venir** (to come) is an irregular verb. It is one of the most used verbs in French and appears in many fixed expressions.

**Present tense conjugation:**
| Pronoun | French | English |
|---|---|---|
| je | **viens** | I come |
| tu | **viens** | you come |
| il/elle/on | **vient** | he/she/one comes |
| nous | **venons** | we come |
| vous | **venez** | you come |
| ils/elles | **viennent** | they come |

**Key points:**
- The stem changes: *vien-* (singular + ils/elles) vs *ven-* (nous/vous)
- 'Viennent' has a double 'n' — listen carefully!

**Common uses:**

1. **To come (movement)**
 - *Je viens à Paris.* = I'm coming to Paris.
 - *Il vient avec nous.* = He's coming with us.

2. **To come from (origin) — venir de + place**
 - *Je viens de Londres.* = I come from London.
 - *Elle vient de France.* = She comes from France.

3. **'D'où viens-tu ?'** = Where are you from / where do you come from?

**Note:** 'Venir de + infinitive' (recent past) is covered in the Advanced track.

examples.english: I have just arrived in town.
They are coming with us tomorrow.

drills.question: Which is the correct form: 'She comes from Lyon'?
drills.explanation: Il/elle → 'vient'. The singular stem is 'vien-': je viens, tu viens, il/elle vient.

drills.question: Complete: Nous ___ de Paris. (We come from Paris.)
drills.explanation: Nous → 'venons'. The nous/vous forms use the stem 'ven-': nous venons, vous venez.

drills.question: Which question asks 'Where are you from?'?
drills.explanation: 'D'où viens-tu ?' = Where do you come from? (formal inversion). 'De' contracts with 'où' to imply origin.

drills.question: Complete: Ils ___ avec nous. (They are coming with us.)
drills.explanation: Ils/elles → 'viennent'. Double 'n' — this is the irregular plural stem: viennent.

drills.question: How do you say 'I come from England'?
drills.explanation: 'Venir de' = to come from. Before a vowel: 'de' becomes 'd'': je viens d'Angleterre.

advanced.title: Venir — Present, Venir de + Infinitive (Recent Past) & Common Expressions
advanced.explanation:
**Venir** is a key irregular verb with several important grammatical functions.

**Present tense:**
| Pronoun | French |
|---|---|
| je | **viens** |
| tu | **viens** |
| il/elle/on | **vient** |
| nous | **venons** |
| vous | **venez** |
| ils/elles | **viennent** |

Stem changes: *vien-* (singular + ils/elles) / *ven-* (nous, vous)

---

**1. Venir de + place (origin)**
- *Je viens de Paris.* = I come from Paris.
- *Elle vient du Japon.* = She comes from Japan. ('du' = de + le)
- *Ils viennent des États-Unis.* = They come from the United States. ('des' = de + les)

**2. Venir de + infinitive (recent past)**
This construction replaces English 'have just done something':
- *Je viens d'arriver.* = I have just arrived.
- *Il vient de partir.* = He has just left.
- *Nous venons de finir.* = We have just finished.
- *Elles viennent de rentrer.* = They have just come home.

> This is one of the most natural ways to talk about something that happened moments ago. There is no equivalent one-word tense — you must use this construction.

**3. Common expressions with venir**
- *D'où viens-tu / venez-vous ?* = Where are you from?
- *Venir chercher quelqu'un* = to come and pick someone up
- *D'où vient ce mot ?* = Where does this word come from? (origin of a concept)
- *Ça vient !* = It's coming! (used by waiters, etc.)
- *Venir à bout de…* = to manage to finish / overcome something

**4. Related verb: revenir (to come back)**
- *Je reviens dans cinq minutes.* = I'll be back in five minutes.
- *Quand reviens-tu ?* = When are you coming back?

examples.french: Je viens de trouver la pharmacie.
examples.english: I have just found the pharmacy.

examples.french: Il vient de partir — tu l'as manqué de peu.
examples.english: He has just left — you just missed him.

examples.french: Nous venons de traverser la place.
examples.english: We have just crossed the square.

examples.french: D'où vient-elle ? — Elle vient du Canada.
examples.english: Where is she from? — She is from Canada.

drills.question: How do you say 'She has just arrived'?
drills.explanation: 'Venir de + infinitive' = recent past. Elle vient d'arriver = she has just arrived.

drills.question: Complete: Nous venons de ___ la place. (We have just crossed the square.)
drills.explanation: 'Venir de + infinitive' expresses recent past. The infinitive follows: venons de traverser.

drills.question: How do you say 'They come from the United States'?
drills.explanation: 'Des' = de + les. Ils viennent des États-Unis (plural country with definite article).

drills.question: Complete: Il vient de ___. (He has just left.)
drills.explanation: 'Venir de partir' = to have just left. Use the infinitive after 'venir de'.

drills.question: Which sentence uses 'venir de' to express recent past correctly?
options: Je viens de finir de travailler.
Je suis venu de finir de travailler.
drills.correctAnswer: Je viens de finir de travailler.
drills.explanation: 'Je viens de finir' = I have just finished. 'Venir de' + infinitive, always 'de' before the verb.

drills.question: Complete: Je ___ dans cinq minutes. (I'll be back in five minutes.)
drills.explanation: 'Revenir' = to come back. Je reviens = I'll be back / I'm coming back (present for near future).

drills.question: How do you ask 'Where does she come from?' (formal)?
drills.explanation: Formal inversion: D'où vient-elle ? The question word 'd'où' (from where) always precedes the inverted verb.

drills.question: Complete: Elles viennent de ___. (They have just come home — rentrer.)
drills.explanation: 'Venir de rentrer' = to have just come home. Elles viennent de rentrer.

standard.question: What does 'tout droit' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Tout droit' = straight ahead. 'Allez tout droit' = Go straight ahead.

standard.question: How do you say 'She comes from Lyon'?
standard.explanation: 'Venir de + place' = to come from. Elle vient de Lyon.

standard.question: Complete: Tournez ___ gauche au carrefour. (Turn left at the crossroads.)
standard.explanation: 'À gauche' = to the left / on the left. Always 'à' before gauche/droite.

standard.question: Which form of 'venir' goes with 'ils'?
standard.explanation: Ils/elles → 'viennent' (double n). This is the irregular plural form.

standard.question: What does 'le plan de la ville' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le plan de la ville' = the city map. Distinct from 'la carte' (larger geographical map).

advanced.question: How do you say 'I have just crossed the bridge'?
options: Je viens de traverser le pont.
advanced.correctAnswer: Je viens de traverser le pont.
advanced.explanation: 'Venir de + infinitive' = recent past. Je viens de traverser le pont = I have just crossed the bridge.

advanced.question: Complete: Continuez ___ droit jusqu'au feu. (Continue straight ahead to the lights.)
advanced.explanation: 'Tout droit' = straight ahead. The full expression is always 'tout droit'.

advanced.question: What does 'venir de + infinitive' express?
advanced.explanation: 'Venir de + infinitive' expresses the recent past — something that has just happened.

advanced.question: How do you say 'She comes from Japan'?
advanced.explanation: 'Du' = de + le. Before masculine countries: venir du + country. Elle vient du Japon.

advanced.question: Complete: Prenez la deuxième rue à ___. (Take the second street on the right.)
advanced.explanation: 'À droite' = on the right / to the right. Always 'à droite' for directions.

advanced.question: Which is the correct formal question for 'Where are you from?'?
advanced.explanation: Formal inversion: D'où venez-vous ? 'D'où' (from where) + inverted verb + subject pronoun.

standard.scenario: A visitor stops you in the street and asks how to get to the nearest pharmacy. Give step-by-step directions using 'tout droit', 'à gauche', 'à droite', and 'venir de'.
keyPhrases: Allez tout droit jusqu'à… (Go straight ahead as far as…)
Tournez à gauche / à droite. (Turn left / right.)
La pharmacie est à gauche / à droite. (The pharmacy is on the left / right.)
C'est à cinq minutes à pied. (It's five minutes on foot.)
Vous ne pouvez pas la manquer. (You can't miss it.)
standard.modelAnswer: Bonjour ! Vous allez tout droit jusqu'au carrefour. Ensuite, vous tournez à gauche. Continuez tout droit — la pharmacie est à droite, après le parc. C'est à cinq minutes à pied. Vous ne pouvez pas la manquer !
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'tout droit' with 'jusqu'au', gives a left turn at the crossroads, continues with direction words, specifies the landmark (the park), gives a time estimate, and ends with a reassuring phrase.
standard.tip: String directions together with 'ensuite' (then) and 'puis' (then/next) to make your instructions clear and easy to follow.

advanced.scenario: A visitor asks you for directions to the nearest pharmacy. Use 'venir de + infinitive' to acknowledge their recent arrival, give detailed step-by-step directions using all direction vocabulary, and ask where they have come from to tailor your answer.
keyPhrases: Vous venez d'arriver ? (Have you just arrived?)
Je viens de… / Vous venez de… (I have just… / You have just…)
Traversez la place / le carrefour. (Cross the square / the crossroads.)
Continuez tout droit jusqu'au feu. (Continue straight ahead to the lights.)
Prenez la direction de… (Take the direction of…)
advanced.modelAnswer: Vous venez d'arriver en ville ? D'où venez-vous — de la gare ? Parfait. Continuez tout droit jusqu'au feu rouge, puis traversez la place. Prenez la direction du marché — c'est indiqué sur le plan là-bas. Tournez à droite, et la pharmacie est à gauche, à deux minutes. Vous venez de faire le plus dur !
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer opens with 'venir de + infinitive' to check recent arrival, uses 'D'où venez-vous ?' for origin, gives detailed sequential directions with traverser/continuer/tourner, references the town map, and closes with a humorous use of 'venir de faire' (you've just done the hardest part).
